critique.summary: The article introduces ripgrep, a command line search tool that claims to be faster and more feature-rich than other popular search tools like grep, ag, git grep, ucg, pt, and sift. It presents detailed benchmarks comparing ripgrep's performance and features, emphasizing its speed, Unicode support, and smart file searching capabilities.
mainPoints: ripgrep combines the usability of The Silver Searcher with the raw performance of GNU grep and is written in Rust.
It supports recursive directory search, respects .gitignore files, ignores hidden and binary files by default, and has full Unicode support without performance penalties.
Benchmarks show ripgrep generally outperforms or matches the speed of other tools in various search scenarios, including large directories and single large files.
ripgrep uses a fast regex engine based on finite automata, avoids backtracking issues seen in PCRE-based tools, and employs literal optimizations and parallelism for speed.
The article discusses the trade-offs of memory mapping versus buffered reading, with ripgrep favoring buffered reading for better performance.
ripgrep supports advanced features like PCRE2 regex engine usage, searching compressed files, and custom file type matching.
The author acknowledges some edge cases where ripgrep may not be fastest and invites users to report issues for investigation.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are largely accurate and supported by detailed benchmark data and technical explanations. Ripgrep is indeed a well-regarded tool in the developer community known for its speed and features. The benchmarks are transparent about methodology and limitations, and the author openly discusses potential biases and invites reproducibility. The technical descriptions of regex engines and file searching strategies align with established knowledge. However, the article is authored by ripgrep's creator, which introduces potential bias, and some claims about being the fastest in all cases are nuanced by benchmark results showing close competitors in certain scenarios.
considerations: The benchmarks are curated and authored by ripgrep's creator, which may introduce bias despite transparency.
Performance can vary based on specific use cases, file types, and system environments; some users report different results.
Other tools may have unique features or compatibility advantages not fully covered in the article.
The article does not deeply explore the impact of very large files or extremely complex regex patterns on performance.
Ripgrep is not POSIX compliant and may not be available or suitable for all platforms or environments.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around ripgrep's performance, usability, and adoption compared to other search tools like grep and ag. Users share personal experiences, praise its speed and documentation, but also mention compatibility issues and occasional frustrations with new tools.

comments.author: drob518
comments.text: I’ve read this multiple times over the years and this post is still the most interesting and informative piece describing the problem of making a fast grep-like tool. I love that it doesn’t just describe how ripgrep works but also how all the other tools work and then compares the various techniques. It’s simultaneously a tutorial and an expert deep dive. Just a beautiful piece of writing. In a perfect world, all code would be similarly documented.
comments.analysis: The comment accurately highlights the quality and depth of the original post, providing a balanced and well-informed perspective on ripgrep's documentation and design.

comments.author: boyter
comments.text:
Such a good read. I actually went back though it the other day to steal the searching for the least common byte idea out to speed up my search tool https://github.com/boyter/cs which when coupled with the simd upper lower search technique from fzf cut the wall clock runtime by a third.

There was this post from cursor https://cursor.com/blog/fast-regex-search today about building an index for agents due to them hitting a limit on ripgrep, but I’m not sure what codebase they are hitting that warrants it. Especially since they would have to be at 100-200 GB to be getting to 15s of runtime. Unless it’s all matches that is.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a practical example of applying ripgrep's techniques and discusses real-world performance considerations, showing a nuanced understanding of search tool optimization.

comments.author: nikisweeting
comments.text:
Ripgrep is used as the defautl search backend for ArchiveBox, such a good tool. I was on ag (the-silver-searcher) for years before I switched, but haven't gone back since.

There's also RGA (ripgrep-all) which searches binary files like PDFs, ebooks, doc files: https://github.com/phiresky/ripgrep-all
comments.analysis: The comment offers a credible user testimonial and useful information about related tools, contributing positively to the discussion about ripgrep's adoption and capabilities.

comments.author: npn
comments.text:
Faster is not always the best thing. I still remember when vs code changed to ripgrep I had to change my habit using it, before then I can just open vs code to any folder and do something with it, even if the folder contains millions of small text files. It worked fine before, but then rg was picked, and it happily used all of my cpu cores scanning files, made me unable to do anything for awhile.

To be honest I hate all the new rust replacement tools, they introduce new behavior just for the sake of it, it's annoying.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a valid critique about performance trade-offs and user experience, reflecting a common concern about resource usage in modern tools.

comments.author: krick
comments.text: I don't remember why I didn't switch from ag, but I remember it was a conscious decision. I think it had something to do with configuration, rg using implicit '.ignore' file (a super-generic name instead of a proper tool-specific config) or even .gitignore, or something else very much unwarranted, that made it annoying to use. Cannot remember, really, only remember that I spent too much time trying to make it behave and decided it isn't worth it. Anyway, faster is nice, but somehow I don't ever feel that ag is too slow for anything. The switch from the previous one (what was it? ack?) felt like a drastic improvement, but ag vs. rg wasn't much difference to me in practice.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a reasonable user perspective on usability and configuration preferences, highlighting that speed alone may not drive adoption.

comments.author: wolandark
comments.text: and incompatible with grep syntax, which makes it useless to most system admins
comments.analysis: While somewhat overstated, the comment raises a valid point about compatibility issues that can affect adoption among traditional users.

comments.author: wewewedxfgdf
comments.text:
I was using ripgrep once and it had a bug that led me downa terrifying rabbit hole - I can't recall what it was but it involved not being able to find text that absolutely should have been there.

Eventually I was considering rebuilding the machine completely but for some reason after a very long time digging deep into the rabbit hole I tried plain old grep and there was the data exactly where it should have been.

So it's such a vague story but it was a while back - I don't remember the specifics but I sure recall the panic.
comments.analysis: The anecdote suggests a possible ripgrep bug or edge case, but lacks detail and context, limiting its usefulness.

comments.author: evilturnip
comments.text:
nowgrep is supposedly even faster than ripgrep:

https://x.com/CharlieMQV/status/1972647630653227054
comments.analysis: The claim is unsubstantiated and relies on a single social media link without technical details, reducing credibility.

comments.author: ventana
comments.text: One thing I learned over the years is that the closer my setup is to the default one, the better. I tried switching to the latest and greatest replacements, such as ack or ripgrep for grep, or httpie for curl, just to always return to the default options. Often, the return was caused by a frustration of not having the new tools installed on the random server I sshed to. It's probably just me being unable to persevere in keeping my environment customized, and I'm happy to see these alternative tools evolve and work for other people.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects a common practical concern about tool adoption and environment management, providing useful context.

comments.author: chriswep
comments.text: It seems to me that `rg` is the number one most important part that enables LLMs to be smart agents in a codebase. Who would have thought that a code search tool would enable AGI?
comments.analysis: The comment exaggerates ripgrep's role in AI development, mixing enthusiasm with hyperbole, thus lacking factual accuracy.
